question: CP violation was discovered in 1964 in neutral kaon decays. What was the signal?

options: Cronin-Fitch experiment: long-lived $K_L$ (mostly CP-odd) observed to decay to $\pi^+\pi^-$ (CP-even final state) at branching $\sim 2\times 10^{-3}$; would be forbidden if CP exactly conserved; established CP violation in nature, parameter $|\varepsilon_K| \approx 2.23 \times 10^{-3}$
Direct observation of antimatter creation from CP violation (not supported by the evidence) (a common misconception) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
Asymmetric pion angular distribution in $K^+ \to \pi^+\pi^0$ (an outdated view superseded by later work) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
$K_L$ never decays â€” CP would be violated if it did (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this conflates cause and effect) (this describes a different phenomenon)
passive.explanation: Strong eigenstates: $K^0(d\bar s), \bar K^0(\bar d s)$. CP eigenstates: $K_1 = (K^0 - \bar K^0)/\sqrt 2$ (CP-even), $K_2 = (K^0 + \bar K^0)/\sqrt 2$ (CP-odd). $\pi\pi$ is CP-even, so $K_2$ can't decay there if CP conserved; goes to $3\pi$ (phase-space suppressed, longer lifetime). $K_L$ = mostly $K_2$ + small admixture $\varepsilon$ of $K_1$. Cronin-Fitch (1964, Nobel 1980): at 17 m from target (much longer than $K_S$ decay length), still see $\pi^+\pi^-$ at branching $\sim 2\times 10^{-3}$ â€” direct CP violation in mixing. Modern: $\varepsilon_K = (2.228 \pm 0.011)\times 10^{-3} e^{i\phi_\varepsilon}$, $\phi_\varepsilon \approx 43.5Â°$.

active.prompt: Match each type of CP violation with its physical meaning:

pairs.definition: $|p/q| \neq 1$; mass eigenstates are not CP eigenstates; characterised by $\varepsilon_K$ in kaons
$|A_f/\bar A_{\bar f}| \neq 1$; rates differ for $P \to f$ vs $\bar P \to \bar f$; $\varepsilon'_K, A_{CP}(B \to K\pi)$
Between mixing and decay amplitudes; $\sin 2\beta$ measured in $B \to J/\psi K_S$
Necessary for matter-antimatter asymmetry: B violation + C/CP violation + departure from thermal equilibrium

active.explanation: Modern CP-violation measurements span K, B, B_s, D systems. Both indirect ($\varepsilon_K$) and direct ($\varepsilon'/\varepsilon \approx 1.7\times 10^{-3}$) CPV established in kaons. B factories (BaBar, Belle 1999-2010, Belle II 2018+): $\sin 2\beta = 0.70 \pm 0.02$ from $B \to J/\psi K_S$; LHCb $\gamma$ from $B \to DK$. CPV in $D \to KK, \pi\pi$: $A_{CP} \sim 1.5\times 10^{-3}$ (LHCb 2019), at edge of SM predictions.

question: Sakharov conditions for baryogenesis (1967): what are the three?

options: (1) Baryon number violation (otherwise net B remains zero); (2) C and CP violation (otherwise opposite-sign asymmetries cancel); (3) Departure from thermal equilibrium (otherwise CPT-related forward/backward rates equilibrate). All three necessary for net matter asymmetry from initial B=0 universe
Just (1) CP violation alone suffices (true only as a special case, not in general) (a common misconception) (this describes a different phenomenon) (this reverses the actual relationship)
(1) Energy conservation, (2) momentum conservation, (3) angular momentum conservation (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (not supported by the evidence)
Sakharov conditions: 3 generations, 3 colours, 3 spatial dimensions (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
passive.explanation: Sakharov (1967) recognised matter-antimatter asymmetry of universe ($n_B/n_\gamma \approx 6\times 10^{-10}$) needs dynamical explanation. (1) B violation: SM electroweak sphalerons preserve B-L but violate B+L; GUTs predict B violation. (2) C and CP: ensures matter and antimatter rates differ. (3) Out of equilibrium: in equilibrium CPT relates particle/antiparticle distributions, so net B remains zero. SM has all three in principle (sphalerons, CKM phase, electroweak phase transition) â€” but quantitatively SM is short: CKM CP gives $\eta_B \sim 10^{-20}$ vs.\ observed $10^{-10}$; EW transition is crossover (not 1st order) for $m_h = 125$ GeV â€” insufficient out-of-equilibrium dynamics. Therefore: BSM CP violation needed. Leading candidates: leptogenesis via heavy $N_R$ decays, EW baryogenesis in BSM extensions.

question: The 'golden mode' $B^0 \to J/\psi K_S$ measures $\sin 2\beta$ cleanly. Why?

options: Both $B^0$ and $\bar B^0$ can decay to common CP eigenstate $J/\psi K_S$; interference between direct decay and mixing-then-decay produces time-dependent CP asymmetry $A_{CP}(t) = \sin 2\beta\sin(\Delta m_d t)$. Tree-dominated decay (no penguin pollution), known phase $\arg(V_{cb}V_{cd}^*)$ â€” measures $\beta$ cleanly with small hadronic uncertainty
Only $B^0$ decays to $J/\psi K_S$; mode is forbidden for $\bar B^0$ (a common misconception) (this reverses the actual relationship) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
Mode dominated by penguin diagrams with unknown phases (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this describes a different phenomenon) (true only as a special case, not in general)
Phase information lost in $K_S \to \pi^+\pi^-$ â€” only rate measurable (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this conflates cause and effect) (not supported by the evidence)
passive.explanation: Mechanism: $B^0$ produced at $\Upsilon(4S)$ or at LHC, mixes to $\bar B^0$ via box diagrams (phase $e^{-2i\beta}$ from $V_{td}^2$ piece); both $B$ and $\bar B$ can decay to $J/\psi K_S$ via tree $b \to c\bar cs$ (phase $\arg V_{cb}V_{cs}^*\approx 0$); interference of paths gives CP asymmetry. Penguin contribution suppressed by GIM. Decay amplitude $A_f/\bar A_f$ has CP-violating phase from CKM. Time-dependent asymmetry: $A_{CP}(t) = -\eta_f \sin(2\beta)\sin(\Delta m_d t)$ where $\eta_f = -1$ for $J/\psi K_S$. Measurements: BaBar (2001), Belle (2001), LHCb: combined $\sin 2\beta = 0.699 \pm 0.017$ â€” currently consistent with global CKM fit. Foundation of $B$-factory program.

question: $\varepsilon'/\varepsilon$ measures direct CP violation in $K\to\pi\pi$. Why is it hard to predict?

options: Strong cancellation between two amplitudes (gluon penguin $Q_6$ contributing $\sim$+1 vs electroweak penguin $Q_8$ contributing $\sim -0.5$, in suitable units) makes prediction depend on precise hadronic matrix elements (B-parameters $B_6, B_8$). Lattice + ChPT calculations have ~30% uncertainty; current prediction $\varepsilon'/\varepsilon \approx (1-2)\times 10^{-3}$, agreeing with experimental $(16.6 \pm 2.3)\times 10^{-4}$ within uncertainties

No cancellation; prediction is straightforward (this describes a different phenomenon) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (not supported by the evidence) (this reverses the actual relationship) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)

Requires perturbative QCD to all orders (true only as a special case, not in general) (this conflates cause and effect) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (a common misconception)

$\varepsilon'/\varepsilon$ is purely kinematic (this describes a different phenomenon) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (not supported by the evidence) (this reverses the actual relationship) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
passive.explanation: $\varepsilon'/\varepsilon$ measures CP violation in decay amplitude (versus $\varepsilon$ in mixing). Effective $\Delta S = 1$ Hamiltonian has 10 operators $Q_1, ..., Q_{10}$; only QCD penguin $Q_6$ and electroweak penguin $Q_8$ contribute significantly. Strong cancellation: $Q_6$ has $\Im(V_{td}V_{ts}^*) > 0$ contribution while $Q_8$ has $\Im(V_{td}V_{ts}^*) < 0$ (different sign of weak operator coefficients), so $\varepsilon'/\varepsilon \propto (B_6 - 0.4\cdot B_8)$ â€” bag parameters $B_6, B_8$ encode hadronic matrix elements, computed in lattice QCD. NA48 (CERN) + KTeV (Fermilab) experimental measurement (1999): $\varepsilon'/\varepsilon = (1.66\pm 0.23)\times 10^{-3}$ â€” first observation of direct CPV. Theory now consistent within ~$1\sigma$ after improved lattice (RBC/UKQCD, 2020+).

question: Electric dipole moments (EDMs) probe CP violation in T-violating interactions. Why are they so sensitive?

options: EDM $d$ is T-odd, P-odd â†’ CP-odd by CPT; SM CKM contributions appear only at 3-loop, predicting $d_e \sim 10^{-38}$ eÂ·cm, $d_n \sim 10^{-32}$ eÂ·cm â€” orders below current bounds. Most BSM models predict EDMs at 1- or 2-loop, much larger. Best limits: $|d_e| < 1.1\times 10^{-29}$ eÂ·cm (ACME ThO 2018, JILA HfF$^+$ 2023); $|d_n| < 1.8\times 10^{-26}$ eÂ·cm â€” already exclude many BSM scenarios with weak-scale CP phases

EDMs measure ordinary electric dipole moment of dielectrics (this reverses the actual relationship) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this describes a different phenomenon) (not supported by the evidence)

EDMs proportional to particle mass; only useful for heavy atoms (true only as a special case, not in general) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this conflates cause and effect) (this confuses two distinct concepts)

SM predicts $d_e \sim 10^{-20}$ eÂ·cm; not very sensitive (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (a common misconception) (this reverses the actual relationship) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: EDM operator $\mathcal H = -d\,\mathbf S\cdot\mathbf E$ violates P and T (hence CP). For elementary particle EDMs in SM: must combine charged current + neutral current + flavour-changing â€” first nonzero contribution at 3 (electron) or 4 (neutron) loops, with strong GIM-like suppression. Predictions: $d_e \sim 10^{-38}$, $d_n \sim 10^{-32}$ eÂ·cm. Most BSM (SUSY, leptoquarks, 2HDM): generically generates EDM at 1-loop with new CP phases, giving $d \sim 10^{-25}\sin\phi_{CP}$ eÂ·cm at weak scale. Experimental bounds from atomic systems (Cs, Hg, Xe, Tl, ThO, HfF$^+$, neutron storage, future muon storage rings). ACME II (2018) $|d_e| < 1.1\times 10^{-29}$ eÂ·cm rules out many SUSY benchmarks. Strong CP problem: $\bar\theta < 10^{-10}$ from neutron EDM.

question: Strong CP problem: why is $\bar\theta$ in QCD so small, and what's the leading solution?

options: QCD allows $\theta$-term $(\theta g^2/32\pi^2)G\tilde G$ generating CP violation; combined with weak-interaction Yukawa phase gives physical $\bar\theta = \theta + \arg\det M_q$; would give $d_n \sim 10^{-16}\bar\theta$ eÂ·cm but experiment $d_n < 1.8\times 10^{-26}$ â†’ $\bar\theta < 10^{-10}$, unnaturally small. Peccei-Quinn: $U(1)_{PQ}$ symmetry spontaneously broken, axion is the pseudo-Goldstone, $\bar\theta$ driven dynamically to zero

$\bar\theta$ is identically zero by gauge invariance (this conflates cause and effect) (not supported by the evidence) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (a common misconception) (an outdated view superseded by later work)

Strong CP problem is solved by $N_c = 3$ alone (this describes a different phenomenon) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (true only as a special case, not in general) (this reverses the actual relationship)

Resolved by chiral symmetry breaking automatically (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this conflates cause and effect) (not supported by the evidence) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (a common misconception)

u})$. CP-violating: $G	ilde G$ is P-odd, T-even â†’ CP-odd. Physical $ ar	heta = 	heta - \arg\det(Y_u Y_d)$ â€” chiral rotations relate gauge and Yukawa contributions. Experimental neutron EDM bound: $ ar	heta \lesssim 10^{-10}$, while naturally $ ar	heta \sim O(1)$. eq 0$). PQ axion may also be dark matter.
